questionText: With reference to temple architecture, Rekha-Prasad, Phamsana and Valabhi are types of which one of the following?
explanation:
● There are many subdivisions of Nagara temples depending on the shape of the Shikhara (Tower). These three major types of Nagara temple architecture are rekha-prasad, phamsana and valabhi.
● There are different names for the various parts of the temple in different parts of India:
○ Rekha-prasada or Latina type: The most common name for the simple shikhara which is square at the base and whose walls curve or slope inward to a point on top is called the 'Latina' or the rekha-prasada type of shikhara.
○ Phamsana type: Phamsana buildings tend to be broader and shorter than Latina ones. Their roofs are composed of several slabs that gently rise to a single point over the center of the building, unlike the Latina ones which look like sharply rising tall towers. Phamsana roofs do not curve inward, instead, they slope upwards on a straight incline. In many North Indian temples, the phamsana design is used for the mandapas while the main garbhagriha is housed in a latina building.
○ Valabhi type: These are rectangular buildings with a roof that rises into a vaulted chamber. The edge of this vaulted chamber is rounded, like the bamboo or wooden wagons that would have been drawn by bullocks in ancient times. They are usually called ‘wagon-vaulted buildings’.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Tomb raised on a square platform
2. Red sandstone and marble used as the building material
3. Garden laid down as a square subdivided into four equal parts
4. Stone carvings in high and low relief on the walls
How many of the above are the characteristic features of the Taj Mahal?
explanation:
● Taj Mahal is a mausoleum of white marble, built in Agra between 1631 and 1648 by order of the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan.
● It is a perfectly symmetrical planned building, with an emphasis on bilateral symmetry along a central axis on which the main features are placed.
● The tomb is placed at one end of the quadripartite garden rather than in the exact centre, which adds rich depth and perspective to the distant view of the monument. The tomb is further raised on a square platform with the four sides of the octagonal base of the minarets extended beyond the square at the corners. So, point 1 is correct.
● The building material used is brick-in-lime mortar veneered with red sandstone and marble and inlay work of precious/semi-precious stones. So, point 2 is correct.
● The garden in front, known as the char bagh, in its ideal form was laid out as a square subdivided into four equal parts. So, point 3 is correct.
● Four types of embellishments have been used with great effect for the interior and exterior surfaces of the Taj Mahal. These are stone carvings in high and low relief on the walls, the delicate carving of marble into jalis and graceful volutes (spiral ornament on the pillar), and the creation of arabesques with pietra dura (yellow marble, jade and jasper) on walls and tombstones and geometric designs with tessellation. So, point 4 is correct.
● Lastly, the art of calligraphy is used with the inlay of jasper in white marble to write Quranic verses.
So, all four of the above are the characteristic features of the Taj Mahal.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Monument/ Building Architecture Style
1. Basilica of Bom Jesus : Persian and Gothic
2. Gateway of India : Portuguese
3. Supreme Court of India : Neo-classical
How many pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation:
● The Basilica of Bom Jesus is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Goa. It is considered to be one of the best examples of Baroque architecture in India and is one of the oldest churches in Goa. The church is made of laterite and plastered with lime and features a simple and unadorned facade. The interior of the church is adorned with intricate carvings, frescoes and gilded altarpieces. The Basilica of Bom Jesus is considered an important example of the Portuguese architectural style that was introduced in Goa during the colonial period. It blends the architectural traditions of Portugal with Indian elements such as the use of laterite stone, and the incorporation of Indian motifs and design elements into the decor. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
● Indo-Gothic Style, also known as the Victorian style, was a unique blend of Indian, Persian and Gothic styles of architecture. The Gateway of India in Mumbai is a good example of this style, as it features a combination of Gothic arches and domes with Indian elements such as intricate carvings and motifs. The structure is made of yellow basalt and reinforced concrete and features a central dome, four turrets and intricate latticework carved into the basalt stone. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched.
● The building of the Supreme Court of India was designed by the architect Ganesh Bhikaji Deolalikar and is in the neo-classical style of architecture. It has been often described as the “Rome of Hindustan”. There was a focus on circular buildings and an overuse of oriental motifs to realise Western architectural designs. So, pair 3 is correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Mandapeshwar Cave in Maharashtra, consider the following statements:
1. It is a rock-cut shrine dedicated to Shiva.
2. It was converted to a church by the Portuguese.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Mandapeshwar Cave:
● It is an 8th Century rock-cut shrine dedicated to Shiva, located near Mount Poinsur in Mumbai in Maharashtra, India. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The cave is believed to have been built approximately 1500 to 1600 years ago, nearly around the same time as the Jogeshwari caves (which were built between 520-550 AD).
● It was originally a Buddhist cave which was later converted into a Shaivite cave temple. With the coming of the Portuguese, the sculptures were defaced and converted into a church by sealing the walls. It was referred to as Mont Pezir by the Portuguese. So, statement 2 is correct.
● After the Marathas defeated the Portuguese, the church was destroyed and the temple was re-established.
● When the Britishers came, they realized that this place was of sacred importance to the Buddhists, Hindus and Christians. Thus, they built a church dedicated to Notre Dame de la Misericorde above the cave temple.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: The ‘Vanga Style of Architecture’ was patronised by which one of the following dynasties?
explanation:
● Vanga style of architecture is a unique style of architecture that originated in the State of Bengal (comprising the present-day Indian states of West Bengal and parts of Odisha and Bihar). The Vanga style of architecture was patronised by the Pala empire.
● The Pala Empire ruled over Bengal and parts of northeastern India from the 8th to the 12th centuries and was known for its cultural achievements and architectural patronage.
● This style is characterised by its distinctive roof design, which is curved upwards at the ends and has a downward slope in the centre. The roof is often supported by wooden beams and pillars, and the walls are made of laterite stone or bricks. Some of the most famous examples of Vanga-style architecture include the Mahasthangarh Temple in Bogra, Bangladesh, and the Rajbari in Bagerhat, Bangladesh.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Chalukyas introduced new elements in temple building like open porch and carving of auspicious motifs on pillars.
2. Ravana Pahadi cave is an example of Chalukyan architecture.
3. Ladh Khan Temple was built in the Panchayatana style.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Aihole was the capital of the early Chalukyan dynasty (6th to 8th centuries). It is a village on the banks of the Malaprabha river. It is historically famous as the cradle of Hindu temple architecture. There are about 125 temples divided into 22 groups scattered all over the villages and nearby fields.
● Early Chalukyan activity takes the form of rock-cut caves while later activity is of structural temples. The earliest is probably the Ravana Phadi cave at Aihole which is known for its distinctive sculptural style. One of the most important sculptures at the site is of Nataraja. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Ladh Khan Temple shows the experimental nature of temple building by the Chalukyas. It is located south of the Durga Temple. It was built in the Panchayatana style. The temple, built about 450 CE, gets its name from a Muslim prince who converted it into his residence. So, statement 3 is correct.
● Cave No. 16 at Ellora is Kailasha Temple dedicated to Lord Shiva. It was developed under the patronage of Rashtrakuta king Krishna I and was carved out of a monolith, and even has a courtyard. In Cave No. 16, there is also a sculpture on the wall of Kailasha temple depicting Ravana shaking Mount Kailash.
● The dynasty built beautiful structural temples. Clusters of temples have been discovered at Pattadakal, Mahakuteshwar and Aihole. They were built with large stone blocks, set together without the help of mortar. Both nagara and dravida style temples were constructed. There are also specimens which are quite unique and cannot be classified either as nagara or dravida. The Chalukyas introduced new elements like open porch, use of balcony slabs and carving of auspicious motifs and figures on pillars. These later became a regular feature of temple architecture. So, statement 1 is correct.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Indian schools of sculptures:
1. Gandhara School was influenced by Hellenistic sculpture.
2. Mathura school sculptures were created using red sandstone.
3. Amaravati school was patronised by Ikshavaku rulers.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● The Gandhara, Mathura, and Amaravati Schools of sculpture are all important schools of sculpture that developed in ancient India.
● Gandhara art can be divided into two schools - early and later. During the early school, which existed during first and second century A.D., blue-grey schist stone was used to make idols. However, in the later school instead of schist stone, mud, lime, pilaster and stucco was used to make the idols. These idols give a realistic representation of the human figure clearly indicating limbs and other organs of the body.
● The Gandhara School flourished in two stages in the period from 50 BC to 500 AD. While the former school was known for its use of bluish-grey sandstone, the later school used mud and stucco for making the sculptures. The images of Buddha and Bodhisattvas were based on the Greco-Roman pantheon and resembled that of Apollo. The sculptures of Mathura School were made using spotted red sandstone. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Amaravati School and Mathura School of Art were developed indigenously and not influenced by external cultures. Gandhara School was heavily influenced by Greek or Hellenistic sculpture, so it is also known as Indo-Greek art. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The Mathura sculptures are characterized by their sensuous and lively representation of deities, with an emphasis on physical beauty and adornment.
● Both the Gandhara School of Art and the Mathura School of Art were patronised by Kushana rulers. King Kanishka who was the greatest ruler of the Kushana empire encouraged both the Gandhara school of Greco-Buddhist Art and the Mathura school of Hindu art.
● Amravati School of Art: This school developed under the patronage of dynasties like the Satavahanas and Ikshavakus. Both bas-reliefs and free-standing sculptures were produced. Satavahana reliefs have been found at Amaravati where they were used for decorating the stupa. They show the stupa, Jataka tales, scenes from the life of the Buddha and Buddhist symbols like chakra. Compared to the Sunga specimens there was great improvement in technique. The figures are deeply carved and have a natural appearance. According to Huntington, this may be because of the influence of Roman art. The reliefs created under the Ikshavakus show even further improvement. These have been recovered from their capital Nagarjunkonda. They were, again, used for decorating stupas. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Agra Fort
2. Fatehpur Sikri
3. Tomb of Salim Chisti
How many of the above were commissioned/ constructed during the reign of Akbar?
explanation:
● Agra Fort is a historical fort in the city of Agra. It was the main residence of the emperors of the Mughal Dynasty till 1638 when the capital was shifted from Agra to Delhi. The Agra Fort is a UNESCO World Heritage site. It was built by the Mughal emperor Jalal-ud-din Mohammad Akbar in 1565 and completed in 1573. So, point 1 is correct.
● Fatehpur Sikri (near Agra), a city predominantly in red sandstone, was built by the Mughal Emperor Akbar. Buland Darwaza gate is the entrance to Jama Masjid Mosque. Nearby is the marble Tomb of Salim Chishti. Diwan-E-Khas Hall has a carved central pillar. Jodha Bai’s Palace is a mix of Hindu and Mughal styles, next to the 5-story Panch Mahal that overlooks the site. So, point 2 is correct.
● The Tomb of Salim Chisti mausoleum was constructed by Akbar as a mark of his respect for Sheikh Salim Chisti a Sufi saint of the “Chishti Order” during the reign of the Mughal Empire. The Tomb was built during the years 1580 and 1581. So, point 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above were commissioned/constructed during the reign of Akbar.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: The ‘Parrot Lady’ sandstone sculpture is associated with which one of the following?
explanation:
● The Parrot Lady also known as a Naayika (Heroine) is a 900-year-old sandstone sculpture, one of many erotic stone sculptures that were created to adorn the Khajuraho temples, Madhya Pradesh. This three-ft-long exquisitely carved sculpture depicts a woman who is voluptuous, scantily clad, has a parrot on her back, and is immersed in conversation with her pet parrot.
● Khajuraho (Chhatarpur district, Madhya Pradesh) is an ancient city known for its 85 magnificent temples and intricate sculptures, built between 950 and 1050 CE by the Chandela Dynasty. It is famous for its Nagara-style architecture and graceful sculptures of Nayikas (Hindu Mythological female protagonists) and deities. These temples represent various forms of meditation, spiritual teachings, and relationships through stimulating art. Their fine sculptures and exceptional architectural skills make them one of the most stunning UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Kalinga/Odisha style of temple architecture:
1. Its exterior walls are lavishly decorated with intricate carvings, but the interior walls are plain.
2. Its shikharas are almost vertical roofs that suddenly curve inwards sharply.
3. ‘Jagamohan’ is one of its notable features.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Kalinga/Odisha style of temple architecture: In different parts of the Kalinga empire, this distinct style of temple architecture developed. Some of its features were:
○ The exterior walls were lavishly decorated with intricate carvings, but the interior walls were plain. So, statement 1 is correct.
○ There was no use of pillars on the porch. Iron girders were used instead to support the roof.
○ The shikhara in the Odisha school was known as rekha deul. They were almost vertical roofs that suddenly curved inwards sharply. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ The mandap was known as jagamohan in this region. So, statement 3 is correct.
○ The ground plan of the main temple was square.
○ Temples were surrounded by a boundary wall as in the Dravidian style of temple architecture.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
Relevance: Odisha temples representing Kalinga architecture have recently made headlines due to a number of incidents.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Brihadisvara temple of Thanjavur
2. Brihadisvara temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram
3. Airavatesvara temple at Darasuram
How many of the above temples are part of the “Great Living Chola Temples”?
explanation:
● The Great Living Chola Temples were built by kings of the Chola Empire, which streched over all of South India and the neighbouring islands. The site includes three great 11th-12th century Temples: The Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur, the Brihadisvara Temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram and the Airavatesvara Temple at Darasuram. All three temples are dedicated to Lord Shiva.
○ The Brihadisvara temple at Tanjavur marks the greatest achievement of the Chola architects. Known in the inscriptions as Dakshina Meru, its construction was inaugurated by the Chola King, Rajaraja I (985-1012 CE) possibly in the 19th regal year (1003-1004 CE) and consecrated by his own hands in the 25th regal year (1009-1010 CE). It used granite for construction stones that were not locally found in the city of Thanjavur. So, point 1 is correct.
○ The Brihadisvara temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram in the Perambalur district was built for Siva by Rajendra I (1012-1044 CE). The temple has sculptures of exceptional quality. The bronzes of Bhogasakti and Subrahmanya are masterpieces of Chola metal icons. The Saurapitha (Solar altar), the lotus altar with eight deities, is considered auspicious. So, point 2 is correct.
○ The Airavatesvara temple at Darasuram was built by the Chola king Rajaraja II (1143-1173 CE). It is much smaller in size as compared to the Brihadisvara temple at Thanjavur and Gangaikonda Cholapuram. So, point 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above temples are part of the Great Living Chola Temples.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Hoysala temple architecture, consider the following statements:
1. Its temple plans were extremely complex and star-shaped.
2. Its sculptures were intricately carved on soapstones.
3. It does not have any influence from the Dravidian or Nagara style of temple architecture.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Hoysala Architecture:
● The most characteristic feature of Hoysala temples is that they grow extremely complex with so many projecting angles emerging from the previously straightforward square temple, that the plan of these temples starts looking like a star, and is thus known as a stellate-plan. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Since they are made out of soapstone which is a relatively soft stone, the artists were able to carve their sculptures intricately. This can be seen particularly in the jewellery of the gods that adorn their temple walls. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Hoysala temples are sometimes called hybrid or vesara as their unique style seems neither completely Dravida nor Nagara, but somewhere in between. Vesara style combined features of both the Nagara school and Dravidian school and resulted in a hybridised style. So, statement 3 is not correct.
● Halebeedu (formerly known as Dwarasamudra) was the ancient capital of the Hoysalas. The Hoysala dynasty ruled over much of South India for close to 200 years and during this time they built both Hindu as well as Jain temples. The Hoysaleswara Temple or the Halebid Temple poised on a star-shaped base on the lawn is an architectural marvel. This twin-shrined temple is perhaps the largest Shiva temple built by the Hoysalas. Its base consists of 8 rows of friezes carved with elephants, lions, horses and floral scrolls. Its walls are adorned with intricately carved Hindu deities, sages, stylised animals, birds and friezes depicting the life of Hoysala kings.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Unlike Mauryan pillars which are constructed in pieces, the Achaemenian pillars are rock-cut pillars.
2. Mauryan pillars were attached to state buildings while the Achaemenian pillars were independently erected.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● During the reign of Ashoka, the inscription on pillars served as a symbol of the State, to commemorate battle victories and to propagate imperial sermons as well. Examples: Lauria Nandangarh Pillar in Champaran, Sarnath Pillar near Varanasi, etc.
● The Mauryan pillars are rock-cut pillars thus displaying the carver’s skills, whereas the Achaemenian pillars are constructed in pieces by a mason. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The shaft of the Ashokan pillars was monolithic, i.e., they were inscribed from a single piece of stone (mainly chunar sandstones). On top of it lay the capital, which was either lotus-shaped or bell-shaped. The bell-shaped capitals were influenced by the Iranian pillars, as was the highly polished and lustrous finish of the pillars. On the other hand, the shafts of Achaemenian pillars were made up of various pieces of sandstone cemented together.
● The Ashokan pillars were independently erected while the Achaemenian pillars were generally attached to state buildings. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Mamalla Temple Architecture:
1. Its pillars are adorned with Simha vyala images.
2. The free-standing monolithic shrines called ‘Rathas’ (chariots) are one of its features.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● In south India, rock-cut architecture laid the foundation for the Dravidian style of architecture. The Pallavas are credited with the introduction of this style. The evolution of the Pallava style of architecture corresponded with the reigning rulers, viz. Mahendravarman I, Narsimhavarman I, Rajsimhavarman and Aparajita.
● The pillars of the temples are one of the very important features of the Mamalla style. Narsimhavarman I or Mamalla is well known for his temple-building activities. In the Mahendra style, they are thick, heavy and cubical with corbel capitals. As a departure, this style displays their better proportion. They are tall, slender, more ornamented and appear elegant. On their base, we find a leogryph (lion-headed creature) or vyala motif. These simha vyalas are shown in a seated position providing support to the pillars. Hence, this style introduced a very vital feature of the Pallava architecture i.e., the pillars adorned with simha vyala images. So, statement 1 is correct.
● A crucial development in the field of Pallava architecture occurred during this style. It witnessed a steady transition from the rock-cut style to the construction of monolithic structures. A component of Mamalla style of architecture was the free-standing monolithic shrines called ‘Rathas’ (chariots) which were constructed alongside pillared halls. They were generally built of granite stones. It is well-known for the construction of the seven pagodas or the monolithic rathas at Mahabalipuram/Mamallapuram (Kanchipuram district, Tamil Nadu). These rathas are built out of monolithic rock. These are 10 in number out of which the five rathas – commonly known as pancha pandava rathas – are of more significance. These pancha rathas are: the Draupadi ratha, Dharmaraja ratha, Bhima ratha, Arjuna ratha, and Nakul-Sahdeva ratha. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Indus Valley Civilisation (IVC):
1. Banawali had radial streets and oval shaped settlements.
2. Daimabad is the only Indus city without citadel.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Statement 1 is correct: Banawali in Haryana is on the bank of dried-up Saraswati River. Toy plough, barley grains, lapis lazuli, fire altars, etc. have been found here. It was an oval shaped settlement and probably the only city with radial streets.
● Statement 2 is incorrect: Daimabad (Maharashtra) is the southernmost site of IVC. Bronze sculptures including a Bronze Chariot have been discovered here.
○ Chanhudaro (Lancashire of India), located in present day Pakistan, is the only Indus city without a citadel. It's located in Sindh, Pakistan, 130 kilometers (81 miles) south of Mohenjodaro.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Which of the following are the characteristic features of the Nagara style of Temple Architecture?
1. Presence of high boundary walls around temples
2. Presence of assembly halls in front of the principal shrine
3. Presence of water tanks or reservoirs in the temple premises
4. Ambulatory passageway around the sanctum sanctorum
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● From the 5th century AD onwards, a distinct style of temple architecture developed in the Northern part of India, known as the Nagara Style of Architecture. Some of the features of the Nagara style are:
● The temples generally followed the Panchayatana style of temple making, which consisted of subsidiary shrines laid out in a crucified ground plan concerning the principal shrine.
● Unlike the Nagara temples, the Dravidian temples were surrounded by high boundary walls. So, point 1 is not correct.
● Presence of assembly halls or mandaps in front of the principal shrine. So, point 2 is correct.
● Outside the garbhagriha, images of the river goddesses Ganga and Yamuna were placed.
● Generally, there were no water tanks or reservoirs present on the temple premises. So, point 3 is not correct.
○ The presence of a water tank inside the temple enclosure was a unique feature of the Dravidian style.
● The temples were generally built on upraised platforms.
● The porticos had a pillared approach.
● The ambulatory passageway or the pradakshina path around the sanctum sanctorum was covered. So, point 4 is correct.
● Generally, the temple premises did not have elaborate boundary walls or gateways.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Vihars were residential halls for the Buddhist and Jain monks.
2. Chaitya halls were developed to be used as prayer halls.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Vihars were residential halls for the Buddhist and Jain monks and were developed during the time of the Mauryan Empire. The cells and halls are usually provided with raised benches for the use of monks. So, statement 1 is correct.
● A chaitya is a shrine cell with a votive stupa placed in the centre. They have a long rectangular hall ending in a semi-circle at the rear end. The hall has a barrel-vaulted ceiling. The doorway is usually placed facing the votive Stupa. Both the Buddhists and the Jainas built Chaityas and Viharas as places of worship. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: In the context of Nayaka school of temple architecture, the term ‘Prakarms’ denotes:
options: a) Large gopurams at the entrance
b) Huge corridors around the garbhagriha
c) Intricate carvings on the walls
d) Sculptures of mythical creatures present in the temple premises.
explanation:
The Nayaka school of temple architecture: It flourished under the Nayaka rulers in the period between 16th-18th centuries C.E. It is also known as Madurai School. It has many features similar to Dravidian style. It also has some Islamic influence on its architecture.
Features of the Nayaka school are –
● Prakarms or huge corridors in the portico, around the garbhagriha. There are also roofed ambulatory passageways.
● The gopurams built under the Nayaka rulers were very huge. The Meenakshi temple in Madurai has the tallest gopuram in the world. The art of gopuram reached its climax under Nayaka style.
● The temple structure was filled with intricate carvings on the walls.
● Meenakshi Temple of Madurai is one of the examples of this style of architecture.
● Therefore, option (b)is the correct answer.

questionText:
With reference to the Seljuk style of architecture, consider the following statements:
1. It was established by the Khilji Dynasty.
2. It was marked by the use of red sandstone.
3. Only tombs were commissioned during this period.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Seljuk style of architecture: Khilji Dynasty established the Seljuk style of architecture. The constructions of this period were marked by the use of red sandstone. Also, the prominence of the Arcuate style began during this period. Mortar began to be used prominently in all the constructions as a cementing agent. Examples: Alai Darwaza by Ala-ud-din Khilji, Siri Fort, etc. Selijuk style of architecture was also characterized by memorial tombs which were usually octagonal structures with domed roofs. So, statements 1 and 2 are correct.
● Architecture under the Lodi Dynasty: Under the Lodi dynasty, architecture continued to take a back seat. Only tombs were commissioned during this period. However, one important feature of architecture during this period was the introduction of double domes. It consisted of a hollow dome inside the top dome. The tombs built during this phase were hard and bare, without any lavish decorations. They were built in octagonal shapes, with a diameter of roughly 15 metres. They were supported by a sloping verandah. Example: Lodi Gardens, the city of Agra was established by Sikandar Lodi, etc. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Rajputana style of architecture, consider the following statements:
1. Like Mughal architecture, there was heavy use of marble in the constructions of Rajputs.
2. They used the concept of hanging balcony (jharokha).
3. Only palaces and forts were built under Rajputana architectural style.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● The Rajput constructions of the period were also influenced by the Mughal style, but were unique in size and scope of their constructions. They generally undertook the building of imposing palaces and forts. Some of the unique features of Rajput architecture are as follows:
○ They used the concept of hanging balconies (jharokha), which were constructed in all shapes and sizes. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ The cornices were built in the shape of an arch such that the shadow took the shape of a bow.
○ Example, Hawa Mahal in Jaipur.
○ Mughal architecture showcased grandeur, symmetry, and the extensive use of marble, while Rajput architecture emphasized fortification, intricate carvings, and the use of sandstone. Both styles continue to be admired and celebrated as important contributions to Indian architecture. So, statement 1 is incorrect.
● Rajput architecture is an architectural style notable for the forts and palaces of the many Rajput rulers, which are popular tourist attractions, many of the Rajput forts are UNESCO World Heritage Site. However, Rajput architecture represents different types of buildings. These include temples, forts, stepwells, gardens, and palaces.
○ For example: Raj Mandir built by Raja Madhukar Shah from 1554–1591. Jahangir Mahal was built in the 17th century by Vir Singh Deo as a welcome to the Mughal emperor Jahangir. So statement 3 is incorrect.
● Therefore option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Ashokan edicts Location
1. Manshera : Gujarat
2. Rampurva : Nepal
3. Jaugada : Odisha
How many pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Pair 1 is incorrectly matched: Manshera, situated in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, holds significant historical importance due to the presence of the Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka. These fourteen major edicts, positioned alongside one of the ancient trade routes, reflect various aspects of Ashoka's dhamma. Carved onto two rocks atop a hill, these inscriptions are written in the Kharoshthi script, with the text flowing from right to left. This archaeological site provides valuable insights into the religious and philosophical beliefs of the ancient Mauryan Empire under Ashoka's reign.
● Pair 2 is incorrectly matched: Rampurva, located in the West Champaran district of Bihar, is renowned for its archaeological significance, particularly for the discovery of two Ashokan pillars. One pillar is crowned with a majestic lion, while the other features a bull atop its structure. The lion pillar bears inscriptions of the Edicts of Ashoka, offering valuable insights into ancient Indian history and the reign of Emperor Ashoka. In contrast, the bull pillar stands without inscriptions, adding to the intrigue surrounding Rampurva's historical legacy and its connection to the Mauryan Empire.
● Pair 3 is correctly matched: Located in the Ganjam district of Odisha, Jaugada holds historical significance as the site of one of the two Kalinga edicts of Ashoka, the other being at Dhauli. These edicts serve as supplements to the series of fourteen Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka, shedding light on the governance principles established for the newly conquered province of Kalinga. Jaugada's inscription provides valuable insights into the administrative policies and ethical framework implemented by Emperor Ashoka, offering a glimpse into the governance practices of ancient India during the Mauryan period.
● Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
With reference to the Rudreshwara temple, consider the following statements:
1. It is located in the state of Tamil Nadu.
2. It was constructed during the reign of the Pandya dynasty in the 11th century AD.
3. It features decorated beams and dolerite with a distinctive and pyramidal Vimana.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● The Rudreshwara temple was constructed in 1213 AD during the reign of the Kakatiya Empire by Recharla Rudra, a general of Kakatiya king Ganapati Deva. It has been inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list. So, statement 2 is incorrect.
○ It is located at Palampet, Mulugu district, near Warangal in the state of Telangana. The presiding deity here is Ramalingeswara Swamy. It is also known as the Ramappa temple, after the sculptor who executed the work in the temple for 40 years. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The foundation is built with the “sandbox technique”, the flooring is granite and the pillars are basalt. The lower part of the temple is red sandstone while the white gopuram is built with light bricks that reportedly float on water. The building features decorated beams and pillars of carved granite and dolerite with a distinctive and pyramidal Vimana (horizontally stepped tower) made of lightweight porous bricks, so-called ‘floating bricks’, which reduced the weight of the roof structures. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following caves:
1. Bagh Caves
2. Pandavleni caves
3. Ajanta caves
4. Elephanta caves
How many of the above are examples of rock-cut caves?
explanation:
● Bagh Caves:
○ They are a collection of nine rock-cut monuments located on the banks of the Baghani River in Madhya Pradesh’s Dhar district. They were developed around the 6th century A.D. These are known for mural paintings by ancient Indian master artists. So, point 1 is correct.
● Pandavleni Caves:
○ It is a group of 24 rock-cut caves located on a hill on the outskirts of Nashik city. So, point 2 is correct.
○ These caves are a group of old Buddhist caves (B.C.250 - A.D.600).
○ All the caves are great examples of intricate carving and craftsmanship.
● Ajanta Caves:
○ Ajanta is a series of rock-cut caves in the Sahyadri ranges on the Waghora River near Aurangabad in Maharashtra. So, point 3 is correct.
○ There are a total of 29 caves, of which 25 were used as Viharas or residential caves while 4 were used as Chaitya or prayer halls.
○ The caves were probably developed in the period between 200 BC and 650 AD.
● Elephanta Caves:
○ The Elephanta Caves are located on Elephanta Island (otherwise known as the Island of Gharapuri), which features two hillocks separated by a narrow valley.
○ These archaeological remains reveal evidence of occupation from as early as the 2nd century BC.
○ The rock-cut Elephanta Caves were constructed about the mid-5th to 6th centuries AD. So, point 4 is correct.
So, all four of the above are examples of rock-cut caves.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Vijayanagara style of Temple Architecture, consider the following statements:
1. It was influenced by the Indo-Islamic architectural style of Bijapur.
2. It is characterised by the Kalyanamandapam with carved pillars in the temple premises.
3. Varadharaja and Ekamparanatha temples at Kanchipuram were built in this style.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Vijayanagara style of Temple Architecture combined the features of Chola, Hoysalas, Pandyas and Chalukyas architectural styles. Under them, the architectural style began getting influenced by the Indo-Islamic style of Bijapur, which in turn was reflected in the temples built during this period. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The chief characteristics of the Vijayanagara architecture were the construction of tall Raya Gopurams or gateways and the Kalyanamandapam with carved pillars in the temple premises. The sculptures on the pillars were carved with distinctive features. The horse was the most common animal found in these pillars. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The most important temples of the Vijayanagar style were found in the Hampi ruins. Vittalaswamy and Hazara Ramaswamy temples were the best examples of this style. The Varadharaja and Ekamparanatha temples at Kanchipuram also stand as examples of the magnificence of the Vijayanagara style of temple architecture. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the temple of Tungnath, consider the following statements:
1. It is one of the five Panchkedars of Uttarakhand.
2. Adi Shankaracharya is associated with its construction.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The central government issued a notification declaring the temple of Tungnath as a monument of national importance.
● It is located in Rudraprayag, Uttarakhand. It is one of the highest Shiva temple in the world. It is the third of the five Panchkedars (Kedarnath, Madhmaheshwar, Tungnath, Rudranath, Kalpnath) of Uttarakhand.
● The temple is believed to have been built by Adi Shankaracharya.
● Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Which of the following temples were built in the Gupta style of temple architecture?
1. Dashavatara temple at Deogarh
2. Yaksha temple at Padmavati
3. Durga Temple at Aihole
4. Kandariya Mahadeva Temple at Khajuraho
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● The Gupta period reflects creative enthusiasm and deep sense and awareness of beauty. These are evident in the following surviving specimens: Dashavatara temple at Deogarh in Jhansi district (UP), Bhitargaon temple near Kanpur (UP), Vishnu temple at Tigawa (Jabalpur district, MP), Shiva temple at Bhumara (Satna district, MP) and Khoh (Bharatpur district, Rajasthan), Parvati temple at Nachna-Kuthara (Panna district, MP), a temple dedicated to the Sun-god at Ashramaka (Satna district, MP) and Mandsaur (MP), Yaksha temple at Padmavati (modern Pawaya) near Gwalior (MP), the temple at Bhitari (Ghazipur district, UP). So, point 1 and point 2 are correct.
● Durga Temple at Aihole, Karnataka was built in the 7th-8th century by the Chalukya Dynasty. So, point 3 is not correct.
● Kandariya Mahadeva temple is known as the most elaborate and the idol Nagara style temple. It was built by Chandela ruler Vidyadhara in the 11th century AD. So, point 4 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Buddhism
2. Hinduism
3. Jainism
How many of the above religions are represented by a group of thirty-four caves at Ellora?
explanation:
● An important cave site located in Aurangabad District of Maharashtra is Ellora. It is located a hundred kilometres from Ajanta and has thirty-four Buddhist, Hindu and Jain caves.
● It has monasteries associated with the three religions dating from the fifth century CE onwards to the eleventh century CE. It is also unique in terms of stylistic eclecticism, i.e., the confluence of many styles in one place. The caves of Ellora and Aurangabad show the ongoing differences between the two religions—Buddhism and Hinduism. Twelve Buddhist caves have many images belonging to Vajrayana Buddhism like Tara, Mahamayuri, Akshobhya, Avalokiteshwara, Maitrya, Amitabha, etc.
So, all three of the above religions are represented by a group of thirty-four caves at Ellora.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: With reference to Indian History, ‘Hathigumpha inscription’ is known for:
options: a) Earliest epigraphic evidence of vishti or forced labour
b) Providing information about Kalinga ruler Kharavela
c) First inscription of Emperor Ashoka that mentioned the name Ashoka
d) Tracing the genealogy of Harshavardhana
explanation:
● Hathigumpha inscription provides information about Kalinga ruler Kharavela. It was written by Raja Kharavela, consists of 17 lines incised in deep cut Brahmi letters and mainly mentions the various conquests of this king. Udayagiri caves are famous for the Hathigumpha inscription which is carved out in Brahmi script.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Lepakshi temple:
1. It has shrines dedicated to Shiva and Vishnu.
2. The Hanging Pillar in the temple does not rest on the ground.
3. The largest monolithic stone statue of Nandi, known as Basavanna, is located in this temple.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Lepakshi is a small village located in the Anantapur District, in Andhra Pradesh. The famous Veerabhadra temple which dates back to 1583, and is dedicated to Veerabhadra, is located here. Built in the 16th century, it is a significant example of Vijayanagar architecture and is characterised by huge granite statues.
● The presiding deity of the temple is Veerabhadra, a fearsome form of Shiva. It has shrines dedicated to Shiva, Vishnu, Papanatheswara, Raghunatha, Rama and other deities. So, statement 1 is correct.
● One of the major highlights of the temple is the Hanging Pillar, which seems like the 70 other pillars in the vicinity but does not rest on the ground. So, statement 2 is correct.
● A gigantic statue of Nandi, also known as Basavanna, is located about 200 mts away from the temple. The seated bull faces the Nagalingeshwara sculpture inside the temple. It is decorated with a necklace and a garland of bells. At a height of 15 ft and a length of 27 ft, it is the largest monolithic stone statue of Nandi in the country. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
In the context of the medieval Indian architecture, consider the following statements:
1. Stone was the primary building material in Trabeate style of architecture.
2. The Arcuate style of architecture is characterized by the use of shikhara.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Statement 1 is correct: Arcuate style of architecture started to use brick, lime plaster and mortar for the constructions. Stone was primarily used in Trabeate style of architecture. Arcuate style was also characterized by the use of arches and domes. They also used minars in the premises.
● Statement 2 is incorrect. The Trabeate style of architecture is characterized by the use of shikhara on top of the temples. They were generally conical in shape. There was also the use of lintels. Minars were absent from this type of architecture.
● Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Amaravati school of art:
1. It was patronized by Satavahana rulers.
2. It depicts the life stories of Buddha and the Jataka tales.
3. The sculptural remains of Amaravati Stupa were created by using red sandstone.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
● Amaravati school of art developed in the eastern Deccan in the lower valleys of the Krishna and the Godavari. The most productive and prosperous phase of this art has been dated to 150 8.C.-350 A.D. Amaravati school was patronised by Satavahana rulers and later by Ikshvaku rulers. Inspired by Buddhist themes the main centres of this art were Nagarjunakonda, Amaravati, Goli, Ghantasala, Jaggayyapeta etc. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The Amaravati school of sculptures are part of narrative art, there is less emphasis on the individual features of Buddha. It depicts the life stories of Buddha and the Jataka tales, i.e., the previous lives of Buddha in both human and animal form. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The sculptures belonging to Amaravati school are found in the valley of Krishna on the sites and museums of Jaggayyapetta, Amaravati and Nagarjunakonda. The collection of the stupa plaques are found at British Museum, London and Government Museum, Chennai. The sculptured panels of Amaravati are characterised by delicacy of forms, and linear grace. Numerous scenes of dance and music adorn these reliefs displayingjoy of life. The sculptural remains of Amaravati Stupa, known as the 'marbles' are created by using white limestone. It creates the illusion of marble and is as fresh as when it was carved. So, statement 3 is not correct.
● Though nature is depicted in the Amaravati school of art, the central characters are human beings. Kings, princes and palaces figure prominently in sculptural representations.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText: With reference to the history of ancient India, the ‘Rummindei’ inscription is known for:
options: a) Ashoka’s visit to the Lumbini
b) Detailed information on the Maurya dynasty
c) Chandragupta’s conquest of the Vanga kingdoms
d) Military expeditions of Emperor Harsha in North India
explanation:
Rummindei Pillar Inscription:
● These inscriptions, written in Brahmi script, come under the Minor Pillar Inscriptions. These contain inscriptions recording their dedication. The inscription mentions Ashoka’s visit to Lumbini (Rummindei), Rupandehi district, Nepal, the birthplace of Lord Buddha. Ashoka exempted Lumbini from paying tax, and fixed its contribution of grain at one – eighth.
● The Rummindei pillars were inspired with cosmological significance- the separation between earth and heaven.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the pre-historic cave paintings, consider the following statements:
1. At Lakhudiyar caves, humans are represented in stick-like forms and there is some superimposition of paintings.
2. The Bhimbetka caves include themes of paintings ranging from mundane events of daily life and royal images.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● First discovery of rock paintings was made in India in 1867–68 by an archaeologist, Archibold Carlleyle, twelve years before the discovery of Altamira in Spain.
● The rock shelters on the banks of the River Suyal at Lakhudiyar bears these prehistoric paintings. Lakhudiyar means one lakh caves. Humans are represented in stick-like forms. There is some superimposition of paintings. The earliest are in black; over these are red ochre paintings and the last group comprises white paintings. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The themes of paintings found at Bhimbetka are of great variety, ranging from mundane events of daily life in those times to sacred and royal images. These include hunting, dancing, music, horse and elephant riders, animal fighting, honey collection, decoration of bodies, and other household scenes. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: With reference to Indian history, the third incarnation of Lord Vishnu in the form of a boar (varaha) rescuing the Earth Goddess from chaos in the presence of adoring gods and saints can be found at which one of the following?
explanation:
● Udayagiri Caves in Vidisha (Madhya Pradesh) are a group of 20 Gupta-era temples and monasteries carved out of a rocky hill, out of which one is dedicated to Jainism and rest to Hinduism.
● Brahmi inscriptions on the caves indicate that the site was excavated during the rule of Gupta King, Chandragupta II.
● The Varaha cave number 5 depicts the third incarnation of Lord Vishnu in the form of a boar (varaha) rescuing the Earth Goddess from chaos in the presence of adoring gods and saints.
● Here Vishnu is represented as a man with a boar's head, placing his left foot on the coils of the Naga king who has a canopy of thirteen snake’s heads.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to different pillar inscriptions of ancient India, consider the following statements:
1. Mehrauli Inscription mentions Ashoka’s human approach to internal administration.
2. In the Besnagar Pillar inscription, Kanishka describes himself as a worshipper of Vishnu.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Statement 1 is incorrect: The rock edicts of Kalsi inscription described Ashoka’s human approach in his internal administration when he converted to Buddhism. It also reflects the policies for the commitment to non-violence and restriction of war. The inscription also tells about Ashoka’s life when he took the path of spirituality. The language of these edicts is Prakrit and the script is Brahmi. The rock edict was written around 450 BC.
○ Mehrauli Inscription: The Mehrauli Iron Pillar is located in Delhi in the Qutub Minar complex. It is notable for the rust-resistant composition of the metals used in its construction. This pillar was established by Chandragupta- II of Gupta dynasty as Vishnupada in the honour of Lord Vishnu. This pillar credits Chandragupta with conquest of the Vanga Countries by his battling alone against the confederacy of the enemies united against him. It also credits him for conquest of Bahlikas in a fight that ran across seven mouths of the Indus River.
● Statement 2 is incorrect: In the Besnagar Pillar inscription, Heliodorus (the Greek ambassador of the Indo-Greek king Antialkidas) describes himself as a Bhagavata i.e. worshiper of Vishnu. Similarly, some coins of Kanishka also show the figure of Siva on them. Thus, we see that many foreign rulers adopted Vaishnavism.
● Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

questionText:
With reference to the famous Bronze Sculpture of ‘Shiva Nataraja’, consider the following statements:
1. It was first created under the patronage of Chalukya Rulers.
2. It is a representation of Lord Shiva performing Ananda Tandava in Tribhanga Mudra.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Shiva Nataraja or Nataraja bronze sculpture represents Lord Shiva balancing himself on his right leg and suppressing the apasmara, the demon of ignorance or forgetfulness with the foot of the same leg. At the same time, he raises his left leg in bhujanga-trasita stance, which represents tirobhava, which is kicking away the veil of maya or illusion from the devotee’s mind. His four arms are outstretched and the main right hand is posed in Abhaya hasta or the gesture suggesting.
○ Archeometric, iconographic and literary evidence shows that the bronze representations of Shiva’s Ananda tandava first appeared in the Pallava period between 7th century CE and mid 9th century CE. Pallava productions of the Nataraja in bronze were borrowed heavily from the Nataraja wooden sculptures. The later Chola craftsmen began experimenting with the greater tensile strength of metal and thus began to produce figures that were flared and dynamic as opposed to the Pallava sculptures that were close-set and linear. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● In the Bronze idol of Shiva Nataraja or Natraja, Shiva is associated with the end of the cosmic world with which this dancing position of Ananda Tandava in Tribhanga Mudra is associated. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Vitthala temple in Karnataka, consider the following statements:
1. It was built during the reign of King Devaraya II of the Vijayanagara Empire.
2. It is built in the Dravidian style of architecture.
3. It is one of the few temples with a stone chariot.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Vitthala Temple in Hampi is an ancient monument that is well-known for its exceptional architecture and unmatched craftsmanship. The temple is located in the north-eastern part of Hampi, near the banks of the Tungabhadra River.
● This temple is dedicated to Lord Vitthala, an incarnation of Lord Vishnu and was built during the reign of King Devaraya II (1422 – 1446 A.D.), one of the rulers of the Vijayanagara Empire. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Several portions of the temple were expanded and enhanced during the reign of Krishnadevaraya (1509 – 1529 A.D.)
● The temple is built in the Dravidian style of architecture. It has traits and features that are characteristic of typical south Indian temple architecture. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The Stone Chariot is considered to be the most stunning architecture of the Vijayanagara Kingdom. It stands in the courtyard of the temple. It is one of the three famous stone chariots in India. The other two chariots are situated in Konark (Odisha) and Mahabalipuram (Tamil Nadu). So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: This Mudra involves both hands. It means turning the Wheel of the Law. This gesture was exhibited by Lord Buddha while he preached the first sermon to a companion after his enlightenment in the Deer Park of Sarnath. This is most likely the description of:
explanation:
Dharmachakra Mudra:
● It means ‘Turning the Wheel of the Dharma or Law’, i.e., setting into motion the wheel of Dharma. This Mudra involves both hands. The right hand is held at chest level with the palm facing outwards. A mystic circle is formed by joining the tips of the index finger and the thumb. The left hand is turned inward and the index finger and thumb of this hand join to touch the right hand’s circle. This gesture was exhibited by Lord Buddha while he preached the first sermon to a companion after his enlightenment in the Deer Park of Sarnath.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
Knowledge Box
● Dhyana Mudra: It indicates Meditation and is also called ‘Samadhi’ or ‘Yoga’ Mudra. It depicts Buddha with both hands in the lap, the back of the right hand resting on the palm of the left hand with fingers extended. In many statues, the thumbs of both hands are shown touching at the tips, thus forming a mystic triangle.
● Vitarka Mudra: It indicates teaching and discussion or intellectual debate. The tips of the thumb and index finger touch each other, forming a circle. The right hand is positioned at shoulder level and the left hand at the hip level, in the lap, with the palm facing upwards.
● Bhumisparsha Mudra: It is one of the most common Mudras found in statues of Buddha. This mudra, formed with all five fingers of the right hand extended to touch the ground, symbolizes the Buddha's enlightenment under the bodhi tree, when he summoned the earth goddess, Sthavara, to bear witness to his attainment of enlightenment.
● The right hand, placed upon the right knee in earth-pressing mudra, and complemented by the left hand which is held flat in the lap in the dhyana mudra of meditation, symbolises the union of method and wisdom, samsara and nirvana, and also the realisation of the conventional and ultimate truths.
Relevance: Recent excavations at Bharatpur in West Bengal have revealed the presence of a Buddhist monastery.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Pottery Period/ Age
1. Painted Grey Ware : Chalcolithic period
2. Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW) : Harappan Age
3. Ochre Coloured : Bronze Age
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● The Painted Grey Ware culture (PGW) is an Iron Age Indo-Aryan culture of the western Gangetic plain and the Ghaggar-Hakra valley in the Indian subcontinent, conventionally dated 1200 to 600–500 BCE. Hence, Pair 1 is incorrect.
● As NBPW was discovered for the first time from the northern part of India; and due to its black color and polished/glazed appearance, it was named as Northern Black Polished Ware. Although the surface color of 90% of NBPW borders around jet black and brownish black, the remaining 10% range between gold, gold-blue. The introduction of NBPW marked the beginning of Second Urbanization in the Ganga plain and it coincides with Buddhist archaeology, introduction of coinage, and art of writing in the middle Ganga plain. (Middle Ganga Plain occupies the eastern portion of the state of Uttar Pradesh and Bihar. Hence, Pair 2 is incorrect.
● The Ochre Coloured Pottery culture (OCP) is a Bronze Age culture of the Indo-Gangetic Plain "generally dated 2000–1500 BCE," extending from eastern Punjab to northeastern Rajasthan and western Uttar Pradesh. The OCP marked the last stage of the North Indian Bronze Age and was succeeded by the Painted Grey Ware culture. Hence, Pair 3 is correct.
● Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer,

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Pietra Dura
2. Red and pink sandstone
3. Charbagh Architecture
4. White and black marble
5. Artificial reservoirs
How many of the above are the features of Mughal Architecture in India?
explanation:
● The first distinct example of proper Mughal architecture inspired by Persian architecture, is the tomb of Humayun, in Delhi, built by his widow, Begha Begum. It stands in the centre of a square garden, divided into 4 main parts by causeways (charbagh) in the centre of which run shallow water channels. The first use of ‘Pink Sandstones’ was seen in this structure. The architecture built during Akbar’s reign shows a blend of the Hindu and Islamic modes of construction ornamentation. The Akbari Mahal and the buildings, along with the city of Fatehpur Sikri, are made of ‘Red Sandstones’. So, points 2 and 3 are correct.
● Taj Mahal is noted for its ethereal and profuse carving and beautiful inlay work with precious multi-colour stones in its floral and arabesque pattern, inscriptions in ‘Black Marbles’, delicate traceries and trellis work with the background of ‘White Marbles’. Like Humayun's tomb, Taj Mahal was also laid in a charbagh style. So, point 4 is correct.
● In 1638, Shahjahan shifted his capital from Agra to Delhi and laid the foundation of Shahjahanabad. Shahjahan commissioned the construction of Red-Fort in 1638 and the construction was completed after 9 years. It is an irregular octagon structure built with red sandstone. It consists of a Diwan-i-Aam, containing a marble canopy ornamented with beautiful panels of ‘Pietra Dura’ work showing a few paintings. Diwan-i-Khas is a high ornamented pillared hall, with a flat ceiling supported on engraved arches. So, point 1 is correct.
● Artificial reservoirs known as ‘baulis’ was the feature of Malwa School of Architecture They were constructed in the premises for storage of water. So, point 5 is not correct.
So, only four of the above are the features of Mughal Architecture in India.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: This Provincial School of Architecture was famous for different coloured stones and marbles. The buildings had large windows, which could be a result of European influence. This School of Architecture, also known as the Pathan School of Architecture’. This is the most likely description of:
explanation:
● Malwa School (1405-1569 AD): The cities of Dhar and Mandu in the Malwa plateau became prominent seats of architecture. The most prominent feature of the buildings here was the use of different coloured stones and marbles. The buildings had large windows, which could be a result of European influence and were decorated by stylised use of arches and pillars.
● Even the stairs were used to enhance the aesthetics of the construction. However, minars were not used in this school of architecture. The Malwa School of Architecture, also known as the Pathan School of Architecture. Examples: Rani Roopmati Pavilion, Jahaz Mahal, Ashrafi Mahal, etc.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.

questionText: Which one of the following is correct with reference to the Tomb of Itmad-ud-daulah?
options: a) The mausoleum was commissioned by Mumtaz, the wife of Shahjahan.
b) It was the first Mughal work made completely of white marble.
c) It introduced the use of shallow cornices and onion dome.
d) It is situated inside the Red fort complex in Delhi.
explanation:
● During the reign of Jahangir, his wife Noor Jahan also undertook some major construction projects like the tomb of Itmad-ud-daulah (father of Noor Jahan) being an excellent example. It was during this time that white marble became the chief building material, replacing red sandstone which had been used till now. The tomb of Itmad-ud-daulah was the first Mughal work made completely of white marble. It also has some of the finest pietra-dura works. Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.
● Tomb of Itmad-ud-daulah is a Mughal mausoleum in the city of Agra in the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh. Often described as a "jewel box", sometimes called the "Bachcha Taj" or the "Baby Taj".
● The Moti Masjid is a 17th-century mosque inside the Red Fort complex in Delhi, India. It was built by Mughal emperor Aurangzeb, damaged by the Siege of Delhi

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sr no. Caves Feature
1. Mandapeshwar caves : Buddhist site
2. Nasik Caves : Hinayana and Mahayana Buddhism influence
3. Junagadh Caves : Jain site
4. Elephanta Caves Panel depicting Ravana shaking Mount Kailash
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Mandapeshwar Caves - Located in Borivalli near Mumbai and also known as Montperir caves, it was developed in the late gupta period as a Brahmanical cave. However, it was later converted into a Christian cave. The remains of the site include sculptures of Natraja, Sada shiva and Ardhanarishwara. The church and its graveyard are situated above the cave precints. Hence, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
● Nasik Caves (Pandavleni caves)- It is a group of 24 Buddhist caves, also known as “Pandav Leni”. They were developed during the 1st century A.D. and belong to the Hinayana period. However, later, the influence of the Mahayana sect can also be found in these caves. Under the Hinayana sect, his presence is indicated through the use of motifs and symbols like throne and footprints. And later, the idols of Buddha were also carved inside these caves representing the influence of Mahayana Buddhism. The site also depicts an excellent system of water management indicated through the presence of water tanks carved out of solid rocks. Hence, pair 2 is correctly matched.
● Junagadh Caves: These are Buddhist caves located in Junagadh district of Gujarat. There are not actually caves here, but three different sites can be found. (1) Khapra Kodiya, (2) Baba Pyare, (3) Uparkot. These caves were carved starting from Emperor Ashoka's period up to 1st–4th century AD. These caves are gilded with beautiful pillars and entrances, water cisterns, horseshoe-shaped chaitya windows, an assembly hall and cell for meditation. Hence, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
● Elephanta Caves: The Elephanta Caves are a collection of cave temples predominantly dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva, which have been designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and a few Buddhist stupa mounds that date back to the 2nd century BCE. Here, The panel facing the Mount Kailash panel towards the northeast corner depicts demon king Ravana trying to lift Kailash and bother Shiva, a legend called Ravananugraha. Hence, pair 4 is correctly matched.
So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Caves at Barabar Hills:
1. These rock-cut chambers bear dedicatory inscriptions in the name of “King Piyadasi”.
2. The caves were used by ascetics from the Ajivika sect.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Barabar Hill Caves are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India, dating from the Maurya Empire (322–185 BCE), some with Ashokan inscriptions, located in the Makhdumpur region of Jehanabad district, Bihar. These caves are situated in the twin hills of Barabar (four caves : Lomus Rishi, Sudama, Viswamitra and Karna Chopar Caves.) and Nagarjuni (three caves). These rock-cut chambers bear dedicatory inscriptions in the name of "King Piyadasi" for the Barabar group, and "Devanampiya Dasaratha" for the Nagarjuni group, thought to date back to the 3rd century BCE during the Maurya period, and to correspond respectively to Ashoka (reigned 273–232 BCE) and his grandson, Dasharatha Maurya. Hence, Statement 1 is correct.
● Ashoka dedicated the caves of Sudama and Visvakarma to the ascetics called "Ajivikas" in the 12th year of his reign, when his religious evolution towards Buddhism was not yet fully completed. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
● Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

questionText: Which one of the following schools of temple architecture has the presence of a step-tank, known as suryakund in the proximity of the temple, as exemplified by the Modhera Sun Temple in Gujarat?
options: a) Pallava School of Temple Architecture
b) Solanki School of Temple Architecture
c) Odisha School of Temple Architecture
d) Khajuraho School of Temple Architecture
explanation:
In the North-Western parts of India including Gujarat and Rajasthan, this school developed under the patronage of Solanki rulers. The features of this school included:
● The temple walls were devoid of any carvings.
● The garbhagriha was connected with the mandapa both internally as well as externally
● The porticos had decorative arched gateways known as torans.
● A unique feature of this school is the presence of a step-tank, known as suryakund in the proximity of the temple.
● The steps of the tank are full of small temples with wooden carvings present in them.
● The Solankis used a variety of material to make temples including sandstone, black basalt and soft-marble
● Most of the temples are east-facing and designed such that every year during the equinoxes, the sun-rays fall directly into the central shrine. Example: Modhera Sun Temple, Gujarat (built in 1026–27 by Bhima-I).
● Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText: With reference to the history of India, the terms ‘Kuta’, ‘ Ayatasra’ and ‘Vrittayata’ denotes:
explanation: ● There are subdivisions also of Dravida temples. These are basically of five different shapes: square, usually called kuta, and also caturasra; rectangular or shala or ayatasra; elliptical, called gaja-prishta or elephant backed, or also called vrittayata, deriving from wagon vaulted shapes of apsidal chaityas with a horse-shoe shaped entrance facade usually called a nasi; circular or vritta; and octagonal or ashtasra. Hence, option (d) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding Jain Pilgrimage Sites in India:
1. The Palitana temples are revered as a sacred site by the Digambar sect.
2. The Gomateswara statue of Lord Bahubali is recognized as the son of Lord Mahavira.
3. Lord Mahavira attained Moksha in Pawapuri, Bihar.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● The Palitana temples, often known only as Palitana, are a large complex of Jain temples located on Shatrunjaya hills near Palitana in Bhavnagar district, Gujarat, India. It is one of the most sacred sites of the Svetambara tradition within Jainism. The main temple is dedicated to Rishabhanatha, the first Tirthankara; it is the holiest shrine for the Svetambara Murtipujaka sect. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect.
● The Gommateshwara statue is a 57-foot (17 m) high monolithic statue on Vindhyagiri Hill in the town of Shravanbelagola in the Indian state of Karnataka. Carved of a single block of granite, it is one of the tallest monolithic statues in the ancient world. It was built around 983 CE during the Western Ganga dynasty and is one of the largest free-standing statues in the world. The Gomateswara statue is of Lord Bahubali, the son of the first Jain Tirthankar. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
● It is believed that the Mahavira passed away and attained moksha (liberation from the cycle of birth and death) at the age of 72 in 468 BC at a place called Pavapuri near modern Rajgir in Bihar. Hence, statement 3 is correct.
● Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
With reference to Satras of Assam, consider the following statements:
1. The institution of Satra was founded by Sankardeva as a part of Vaishnavism in Assam.
2. The Satras serve as monasteries as well as centres of traditional performing arts.
3. Kamalabari, Dakhinpat, Garamur and Samaguri are some of the Satras found in Assam.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● The Institution of Satra is a unique feature of Vaishnavism in Assam, founded by Sankardeva, the father of Assamese culture. The satras are not just monasteries, but centres of traditional performing arts. So, statements 1 and 2 are correct.
● In the 15th century the first Satra was founded in Majuli. Since then sixty five Satras have come up for the propagation of ethics and socio-cultural ideals.
● The satras also offer guest accommodation where devotees and visitors not only take part in worship of Vishnu and Krishna,but also watch traditional bhaona performances. Young bhakats – celibates – are wonderful artisans and make masks, musical instruments as well as hand-fans and door frames. Some of the satras worth visiting are Auni-ati, Kamalabari, Dakhinpat, Garamur, Samaguri, Bengena-ati and Natun Kamalabari. So, statement 3 is correct.
● Therefore, option c is the correct answer.

questionText:
The constructions were anonymous and without any interesting features. It was a confluence of all styles of architecture which made the style congested and cramped the space of artistic expression. There was an overuse of oriental motifs to realise western architectural designs. The concept of an upturned dome was introduced during this phase. The architecture of New Delhi, done by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker were the finest examples of this style.
The above paragraph most suitably describes which of the following styles of architecture?
explanation:
Neo-Roman Style: Post-1911, the constructions undertaken by the British Raj were done according to Neo-Roman style or Neo-Classical style. The architecture of New Delhi, done by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker were the finest examples of this style. It has been often described as the “Rome of Hindustan”. The features of this phase are:
● The constructions were anonymous and without any interesting features.
● It was a confluence of all styles of architecture which made the style congested and cramped the space of artistic expression.
● Simplicity, modernity and utility were highly compromised due to the hybrid nature of the constructions.
● There was a focus on circular buildings.
● There was an overuse of oriental motifs to realise western architectural designs.
● The concept of an upturned dome, as can be seen in the Supreme Court and Rashtrapati Bhavan, was introduced during this phase.
● Therefore, option c is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the Indo-Gothic style of Architecture:
1. It came into existence after 1920 in India.
2. The walls were thicker than those found in the Indo-Islamic constructions.
3. It was a unique blend of Indian, Persian and Gothic styles of architecture.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
● Statement 1 is incorrect: Indo-Gothic style of Architecture emerged in the period before 1911 and is also known as Victorian style.
● Statement 2 is incorrect: In Indo-Gothic style of architecture, the walls were thinner than in the Indo-Islamic constructions.
● Statement 3 is correct: It was a unique blend of Indian, Persian and Gothic styles of architecture. It was the British who brought with them the Gothic style of architecture. It merged with the Indian architecture and resulted in the Indo-Gothic style of architecture.
● Therefore, option a is the correct answer.
